content.assessment: Shrinking the state-sector footprint of selective tracks reduces the number of seats subject to the inequitable selection mechanism, but does not alter the per-seat inequity of that mechanism (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-02), and its jurisdictional limits leave church/private growth—already documented as expanding and selection-capable—outside its reach (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-07).
opportunities: Could reduce aggregate exposure to a system associated with worse outcomes for students who remain in ordinary tracks after selection (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-04)
strengths: Reduces the absolute scale of a track structure linked to higher inequality and reduced mean performance (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-01)
Formalizes an existing administrative lever rather than requiring new legislative architecture, offering a plausible near-term reduction in state-sector selective seats
threats: Church and private schools, exempt from these caps, are already growing their selective share and could absorb displaced demand, offsetting or worsening segregation via relocation to the least-regulated maintainer segment (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-07)
Relocation of sorting to church/private sector could deepen socioeconomic and geographic stratification if those sectors have weaker access equity than the state sector
weaknesses: Leaves the admission criteria's inequity-per-seat entirely unchanged: those admitted still disproportionately come from higher-SES backgrounds (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-02)

Does not address Roma segregation or within-school classroom sorting mechanisms that operate independently of state-sector capacity (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-06, F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-10)
